---
title: "Overview"
---

# Module 3: Inferential Threats & Identification {.unnumbered}

## The "Independent" Variable

A variable only earns the label "independent" after you have done the work to establish that it is exogenously related to your outcome. Until then, it is just a predictor — one that may be deeply entangled with other forces that also shape the outcome.

Nested data creates special entanglement problems. When people are grouped into firms, schools, neighborhoods, or time periods, the things that determine their group membership are often the same things that affect their outcomes. A person does not end up in a high-autonomy firm at random. A student does not end up in a high-quality classroom at random. A country does not adopt a policy at random.

This module is about recognizing these entanglements — **endogeneity** — and learning the identification strategies that let us recover credible causal claims despite them.

## What This Module Covers

**Part 1: Endogeneity** — The three sources of endogeneity in social science data: omitted variable bias, simultaneity/reverse causality, and selection. What each looks like in the DGP, and how to recognize them.

**Part 2: Instrumental Variables** — When you cannot randomize, an instrument can do some of the work. What makes a valid instrument, the two-stage procedure, and real examples from consumer and organizational research.

**Part 3: Selection Effects & Attrition** — The many ways observations end up in your dataset non-randomly: representativeness failures, self-selection, survivorship, attrition in longitudinal studies, and researcher-driven exclusions.

**Part 4: Regression Discontinuity Design** — When treatment assignment is based on a threshold, the continuity of potential outcomes around that threshold gives you a local quasi-experiment. Assumptions, tests, and worked examples.

**Part 5: Difference-in-Differences** — When some units receive a treatment and others do not, and you have pre- and post-treatment observations, DiD recovers causal effects under the parallel trends assumption.

**Part 6: Propensity Scores & Matching** — When treatment is observational but you can condition on all relevant confounders, matching and weighting approaches can reduce bias.

## The Through-Line: Identification

Every method in this module is an **identification strategy** — a way to isolate variation in your predictor that is (approximately) exogenous, and use only that variation to estimate the effect of interest.

The math of each strategy is not the hard part. The hard part is the **judgment**: Does your instrument really satisfy the exclusion restriction? Are your parallel trends really plausible? Can you really claim you have measured all confounders?

This module teaches both the tools and the judgment.
